name: Cova de can Sion
starting_point: Cami Vell de Campanet, 3.4 km from MA-2200 road km 47.230, on the Palma-Alcudia to Pollensa road
equipment: No vertical progression equipment needed, but a rope is helpful for the lower part of the cave. Some unexplored areas may require vertical equipment.
description: Behind its small entrance lies a cave of impressive dimensions and beauty comparable to any other on the island, with abundant concretions, numerous large water-filled gours, and densely packed columns. The cave has two difficulty levels ranging from horizontal to vertical zones. It houses a large bat colony and contains ceramic remains indicating historical human use for water collection. Classified as a ZEC site, access requires permission from the Environmental Ministry.
full_description:
The small mouth hides a cave of great dimensions and a beauty comparable to any other on the island. As soon as we find ourselves inside, we can move in any direction since the cave will always surprise us. On my first visit to this cave, accompanied by my wife, I dedicated myself to visiting the zone on the left side, a completely concretioned place where, especially at the beginning, we will have to use our hands to overcome some of its ramps. We will immediately begin to marvel at its innumerable rimstone pools, some of great dimensions and almost all of them full of water — notably a semi-aerial one shaped like a shell. The columns are packed together one next to another, leaving in some places a small space between them that will serve us to continue the visit.

As I said at the beginning, we can visit the cave in multiple ways. One that I propose could be to start by descending in a straight line from the entrance and visiting the entire right side, which in principle is not very large, and then move to the left side where there is a great room in which large blocks accumulate on the floor.

In short, each person should do it however they wish, since there is plenty of cave to visit. Next to a large flowstone there is a corner through which one continues through a crawlway, descending to a lower level where it is necessary to use a rope to avoid problems on the return, as the water makes it a rather slippery zone. Also on the other side of the cave there is some tricky spot where, depending on each person's ability, a rope may be necessary.

A large colony of bats resides inside the cave, so we must be careful to treat both them and the cave with the utmost respect, trying to avoid startling them, mainly by not shining light directly at them. To give an idea of the quantity of bats that inhabit this place, I can say that I have been able to verify that some piles of guano exceed one meter in height.

This cave, like others, is classified with two difficulty levels, going from a horizontal zone to a vertical one where one must be careful not to proceed if it is not within our capability (before taking a step, make sure). In any case, the dimensions of the cavity are so great that we can get lost for hours enjoying its formations as well as taking photographs.

In a fairly deep zone of the cave, I found quite a few ceramic remains, apparently not of great antiquity, which indicates that, as in other important cavities on the island, humans ventured into the depths of this cave with the intention of obtaining water, mainly during the summer season.

NOTE: Cova Can Sion is one of the 30 ZEC (Special Conservation Zone) cavities that currently exist on the island of Mallorca. For this reason, access is prohibited without the corresponding permit from the Ministry of the Environment.
